name: Sunny Murray
abstract: James Marcellus Arthur "Sunny" Murray (September 21, 1936 – December 7, 2017) was an American musician, and was one of the pioneers of the free jazz style of drumming.

about_quotes.speaker: Rashied Ali and Henry Grimes
about_quotes.evidence: Sunny Murray, which is a friend of mine also from Philly who plays drums
about_quotes.doc_title: Rashied Ali and Henry Grimes interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, January 30, 2009

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee mentions Sunny Murray only in the context of the interviewer's question about historical documentation ('...with Sunny Murray and Jimmy Lyons at Montmartre in 1962'). The interviewee does not mention Sunny Murray in their response, nor do they describe any personal interaction, colla
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Roswell Rudd
about_quotes.evidence: for instance here’s a wild little band, Albert Ayler on the tenor and soprano saxophones with Cecil Taylor on the piano, Sunny Murray on the drums and Louis Worrell on the bass, playing together in a coffee house in Greenwich Village in 1961 or something.
about_quotes.doc_title: Roswell Rudd interviewed by Monk Rowe, Ellenville, New York, June 23, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Sam Jones
about_quotes.evidence: I'll never forget one night Sunny Murray was down there sitting there on the drums... As I asked him as he came off the stage, I said, 'what was all that shit all about, you know.'

about_quotes.speaker: Tyner McCoy
about_quotes.evidence: INTERVIEWER: I know Sunny Murray went back to Philadelphia and drove a cab too so I thought, well maybe this-
about_quotes.doc_title: Tyner McCoy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jon Jang
about_quotes.evidence: I sat in his big band one time and they had Sunny Murray and it was a great, great experience
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon Jang interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, September 17, 2003 [interview 1 of 2]
